text: The surgeon opens the lesser sac and gastrocolic ligament, carefully preserving the gastroepiploic arcade during the abdominal exploration, which shows no evidence of metastatic disease.
Upon entering the lesser sac, the surgeon identifies a cystic lesion within the pancreatic region.
The surgeon mobilizes the stomach, detaching it from the antrum to the gastrosplenic ligament, revealing the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic ultrasound to examine the cystic lesion for vascular involvement, clearly identifying the borders of the mass within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon identifies and isolates the splenic artery and vein from the posterior aspect of the pancreas, utilizing both superior and inferior approaches to access the retropancreatic space.
The surgeon develops the infrapancreatic border and opens the retroperitoneal plane, revealing underlying anatomical structures and facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the site for proximal transection of the pancreas at the level of the portal vein, preparing for the next step in the pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon alternates between supra- and infrapancreatic approaches to access the retropancreatic space at the neck of the pancreas, facilitating the isolation of the splenic artery and vein.
The surgeon meticulously dissects and skeletonizes the periportal region, ensuring all vascular connections between the pancreas and splenic vessels are ligated.
The surgeon creates a retropancreatic tunnel and passes umbilical tape through it to facilitate manipulation of the distal pancreas.
The surgeon further isolates the pancreas from the splenic vasculature, then uses a stapling device to transect the pancreas at the identified proximal site.
The surgeon excises the distal staple line of the pancreas and prepares the tissue for frozen section analysis to confirm negative margins.
The surgeon closes the distal pancreatic duct with sutures, using them to gain traction for further dissection of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the tortuous path of the splenic vessels, using a bipolar vessel sealer to minimize thermal spread and prevent vascular injury.

As the dissection approaches the splenic hilum, the pancreas is observed dropping inferiorly below the hilum. The surgeon carefully delineates the course of the pancreas from the adjacent splenic vessels.
The surgeon oversews the proximal staple line of the pancreas, applying an adhesive polymer to seal the newly exposed distal aspect of the pancreas.
